text: In this video we describe a laparoscopic approach to repair of a complex recurrent incisional hernia. The patient was a fifty six year old lady who presented electively having recently been admitted with an episode of hernia incarceration which was successfully managed conservatively. Her past medical history is outlined in this slide.

The surgeon establishes pneumoperitoneum using a Veress needle and inserts six 5mm ports under direct laparoscopic vision. Dense intra-abdominal adhesions are visible, and adhesiolysis is performed using scissors and a harmonic scalpel. Scissors are used to carefully separate the small bowel from the anterior abdominal wall, while the harmonic scalpel is used for omental adhesions, maintaining a bloodless plane.

The hernial sac is visible on the right of the midline, containing multiple small bowel loops. Band adhesions are carefully divided to return the bowel loops to the peritoneal cavity. The small bowel loops are densely adherent to previously placed mesh, requiring meticulous dissection to avoid enterotomy as they are freed from the anterior abdominal wall.

A second suprapubic hernial defect is visible on the left side of the screen. The surgeon encounters densely adherent small bowel loops, attempting various techniques including hydrodissection to free them. Due to the difficulty in safely removing the bowel, the procedure is converted to an open approach, allowing for safe dissection of the bowel and excision of the hernial sacs, followed by primary closure of the fascial defects.

After re-establishing pneumoperitoneum, the surgeon inserts a 20x30 cm physio mesh into the peritoneal cavity. Stay sutures are placed into the mesh and pulled taut using the endoclaw device to lift the mesh onto the anterior abdominal wall. The mesh is then circumferentially tacked in place with a secure strap device. Transfascial sutures are passed through the abdominal wall and mesh at five-centimeter intervals, ensuring the mesh is securely anchored to the anterior abdominal wall.
The surgeon performs a thorough check for hemostasis and inspects the bowel for any enterotomies or significant serosal tears. All laparoscopic ports are removed under direct vision.
